artifacts.content:
Hi Rach,

Kids are finally down and I’m hiding in the kitchen with the last of the coffee, so I figured I’d actually write you back instead of just liking your pictures.

It’s been a weird, heavy few months. You know about Melissa’s panic attack at Sacred Heart in Spokane on November 3, 2025, but I don’t think I told you how much it shook her. One minute we were in the ER thinking it was her heart, next thing they’re calling it a panic attack and telling us to follow up with Spokane Behavioral Health. She’s been seeing that therapist, Dr. Karen Lewis, and Dr. Lewis keeps saying we’ve got to cut down on money stress because that’s a big trigger. Melissa’s missed a few shifts at the elementary school since then, and every time the phone rings from the school she jumps.

On top of that, I keep thinking about Mom last winter, January–February 2025, when she almost got evicted from her HUD unit on the rez. Remember how close Spokane Tribal Housing Authority was to sending the formal eviction notice? We ended up scraping together about $1,150 to pay Housing and Avista Utilities to get her rent and power caught up. I’m proud we did it, but I’m still kind of mad at myself that we didn’t see it coming sooner.

Day-to-day stuff is the same old. Kayla’s basketball practices are twice a week now, so I’m pacing the Wellpinit gym hallways trying to get my steps in while Jordan drives his toy trucks over every crack in the floor. Subaru’s still got that rattle in the back, but I’m ignoring it until after we get through winter tires and propane.

Anyway, didn’t mean to dump all that. Just wanted you to know what’s been going on up here. How are you holding up with the commute into Spokane every day?

Love,
Dan

To: seniorservices@spokanetribe.gov
Subject: Checking on winter help options for my mom

Hello,

My name is Daniel Redhawk. I’m writing because I’m getting nervous about this winter for my mom, Evelyn Rose Redhawk, and I wanted to ask what programs might be available ahead of time instead of waiting for a crisis.

Last winter (January–February 2025) she fell behind on both rent and power at her HUD-assisted unit on the Spokane Reservation. Spokane Tribal Housing Authority was getting ready to move forward with an eviction, and Avista Utilities had already sent a shutoff notice. My wife and I ended up paying about $1,150 between Housing and Avista to get her caught up so she wouldn’t get a formal eviction notice. We were glad we could do it, but it really strained our budget for a few months.

We already help her as much as we can — usually $150–$200 a month for groceries and utilities — but with prices going up and my own mortgage, auto loan, and Spokane Tribal Credit payments, I’m worried we might not be able to cover another big past-due amount like that if she falls behind again.

Are there any senior-specific funds, LIHEAP-style energy assistance, or rent support programs we should be getting her on a list for now, before the real cold hits? She doesn’t always understand the paperwork, and I don’t want her to wait until she’s already in trouble again.

If it’s easier to talk this through in person, I can stop by the office after my shift at Housing Authority (I’m usually done around 4:00 p.m.).

Thank you for any guidance you can give us.

Sincerely,

Daniel J. Redhawk
Wellpinit, WA

metadata.subject: Winter assistance questions for my mom

artifacts.content:
Kitchen table notes – money + work stuff (don’t lose this)

1. Taxes / side work
- Remember how bad that Washington State Department of Revenue wage garnishment felt back in April–August 2019 – about $95 coming out of every paycheck.
- That was all from doing side jobs in Spokane (porch steps, drywall, etc.) and not filing the state taxes right.
- DO NOT repeat that. Any side work now: keep a running list in the green notebook, set aside at least 25% in savings for state/fed, ask a tax person about quarterly estimates before spring.

2. Job security
- October 2023 written warning from Mark Jensen at Spokane Tribal Housing Authority is STILL in my personnel file.
- Missed three days without proper call-in while we were at Providence Holy Family Hospital in Spokane with Jordan’s asthma flare-up.
- Need to follow the call-in procedure exactly next time, even if we’re in the ER. Ask HR quietly about FMLA options for kids’ health.

3. Family health + stress
- Melissa’s panic attack at Sacred Heart Medical Center in Spokane on 11/3/25 scared both of us. She’s seeing Dr. Karen Lewis at Spokane Behavioral Health.
- Dr. Lewis keeps saying: lower financial stress where we can.

4. Action items
- Call tax preparer in Spokane before end of month – bring 2019 DOR letters, side job notes.
- Email HR about FMLA info (don’t sound panicked, just “planning ahead”).
- Keep at least $1,000 untouched in emergency savings for car/home/medical.
- Re-check budget spreadsheet after payday; look for any subscriptions we can cut.

Tape this inside the laptop so I can’t ignore it.

To: hr@spokanetribalhousing.org
Subject: Question about leave options for family medical issues

Hi,

This is Daniel Redhawk from Facilities Maintenance. I had a question about leave options and wanted to ask before we get into the busy part of winter.

Back in October 2023 I received a written warning from my supervisor, Mark Jensen, because I missed three days of work without following the proper call-in procedure. Those days were when my son, Jordan, had a bad asthma flare-up and we were in the ER at Providence Holy Family Hospital in Spokane. At the time I was just focused on him and honestly didn’t think clearly about the attendance policy.

Jordan’s asthma has been mostly under control since then, but our pediatrician warned us that another serious episode is possible. On top of that, my wife, Melissa, had a panic attack at Sacred Heart Medical Center in Spokane on November 3, 2025, and she’s been seeing Dr. Karen Lewis at Spokane Behavioral Health for follow-up. Between her missing some work and the kids’ health, I’m trying to be proactive so I don’t put my job at risk if we have another emergency.

Could you let me know what options there are for taking time off if I need to be at the hospital with my kids or wife? For example, does FMLA apply in situations like that, and what documentation would you need from doctors or hospitals? I want to make sure I understand the right way to handle it so I don’t end up with another write-up.

I’m happy to stop by the office to talk in person if that’s easier.

Thank you,

Daniel J. Redhawk
Facilities Maintenance Technician

metadata.subject: Question about leave options for family medical issues

artifacts.content:
Thread: Dan & Mike

Dan: You off the river yet or still chasing steelhead? 😄

Mike: Just pulled the boat out. What’s up?

Dan: Been thinking about money crap again. Remember when WA Dept. of Revenue was taking like $95 out of every paycheck from April thru August 2019?

Mike: Yeah, that garnishment sucked. All over those side jobs you did in Spokane.

Dan: Yup. Old unpaid state taxes because I didn’t file right on the side work. Don’t want to ever be back there, especially after last winter with Mom.

Mike: For real. That Jan–Feb 2025 mess was rough.

Dan: Housing was about to evict her from that HUD unit and Avista was ready to shut the power off. We ended up dropping like $1,150 between Spokane Tribal Housing Authority and Avista Utilities just to keep her from getting a formal eviction notice.

Mike: I still don’t know how you and Mel pulled that off.

Dan: Credit cards and a lot of ramen. 😅 Now Melissa’s missing some hours since her panic attack at Sacred Heart on 11/3/25, and Dr. Karen Lewis at Spokane Behavioral Health keeps telling us to cut stress.

Mike: You two can’t catch a break.

Dan: It’s not all bad. Kids are good, Kayla’s hitting her free throws finally, Jordan’s trucks are all over the living room. Just feels like one big bill away from sliding backwards again.

Mike: Let’s sit down this weekend, look at your side job list, make sure you’re setting aside tax money this time.

Dan: Deal. I’ll bring the green notebook and some coffee.

Mike: I’ll bring jerky.

Dan: Now you’re talking.

Title: Melissa – therapy follow-up (Spokane Behavioral Health)

Time: Tuesday, March 10, 2026, 2:30–3:30 p.m.
Location: Spokane Behavioral Health, Spokane, WA
Attendees: Melissa, Daniel (if I can get off early)

Notes:

- This is the follow-up with Dr. Karen Lewis that they scheduled back when Melissa had the panic attack at Sacred Heart Medical Center in Spokane on November 3, 2025.
- Try to leave work by 1:15 p.m. from Spokane Tribal Housing Authority so I can drive the Subaru in, pick her up at the elementary school, and get to the clinic a little early.
- Things to bring up with Dr. Lewis:
  • Melissa’s missed work shifts since November and how the reduced hours are stressing her out.
  • Money worries: mortgage ($1,785), auto loan, Spokane Tribal Credit payments, and helping Mom with utilities.
  • How much the 2019 Washington State Department of Revenue wage garnishment still sits in the back of my mind – scared of messing up again with side jobs.
  • Ask if consolidating some of the higher-interest stuff and getting ahead of winter bills might actually lower her anxiety, or if we’re just rearranging the same stress.
- After appointment: swing by Costco if budget allows – need bulk rice, kids’ snacks, and maybe check prices on water heaters again.
- Text Mom when we’re done to see if she needs anything from Spokane before we head back to Wellpinit.

Reminder: leave a note for Kayla about catching the bus and for Rachel in case we run late and need backup pickup.

To: taxhelp.spokane@gmail.com
Subject: Need help getting side job taxes straight

Hi,

My name is Daniel Redhawk. A friend from Housing Authority gave me your email and said you’re good with folks who do irregular side work.

I want to get ahead of things this year. Back in 2019 I had a wage garnishment from the Washington State Department of Revenue from around April through August. They were taking about $95 out of every paycheck over some old unpaid state taxes from side jobs I did in Spokane (small home repairs, hauling, etc.) that I didn’t file correctly. It’s fully paid off now, but it really wrecked our budget at the time and I don’t want to repeat that.

Our situation is a little tight these days. My wife, Melissa, had a panic attack at Sacred Heart Medical Center in Spokane on November 3, 2025, and she’s been missing some work since then while seeing a therapist, Dr. Karen Lewis, at Spokane Behavioral Health. On top of that, we sometimes have to step in to help my mom, Evelyn, when she falls behind on rent and utilities at her HUD unit on the Spokane Reservation – last winter (Jan–Feb 2025) we paid about $1,150 directly to Spokane Tribal Housing Authority and Avista Utilities to keep her from almost getting evicted.

Given all that, I really need any extra money from side jobs to go toward savings and debt, not surprise tax bills. Could you help me set up a simple system for tracking income/expenses and figuring out what to set aside for state and federal taxes? I’m happy to bring my 2019 DOR letters, last year’s return, and a list of the work I’ve done lately.

If you’re available, evenings after 5:00 p.m. in Spokane or Saturday mornings work best for me.

Thank you,

Daniel J. Redhawk
Wellpinit, WA

metadata.subject: Need help getting side job taxes straight
